channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: [7] Energy-efficient field-free switching by orbital torque and spin-reorientation
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72894-8

Researchers demonstrate field-free magnetisation switching in perpendicular magnetic anisotropy systems using spin reorientation and orbital Hall effects. This approach enables low power operation and scalable spintronic memory devices.
item.dc:title: [7] Energy-efficient field-free switching by orbital torque and spin-reorientation
item.dc:creator: Bilal Jamshed, Subhakanta Das, Pinkesh Kumar Mishra, Wai Lum William Mah, Mathias Kläui, S. N. Piramanayagam
Subhakanta Das
Pinkesh Kumar Mishra
Wai Lum William Mah
Mathias Kläui
S. N. Piramanayagam
item.description:
Last authors: Mathias Kläui, S. N. Piramanayagam

Authors: Bilal Jamshed, Subhakanta Das, Pinkesh Kumar Mishra, Wai Lum William Mah, Mathias Kläui, S. N. Piramanayagam

Score 7/10 B_IMPORTANT_CONDMAT

Why: Spintronics with orbital Hall effect and magnetic switching, relevant to quantum materials.

Tags: #spintronics #magnetism #Halleffect

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72894-8 Researchers demonstrate field-free magnetisation switching in perpendicular magnetic anisotropy systems using spin reorientation and orbital Hall effects. This approach enables low power operation and scalable spintronic memory devices.
